title: Combining two colonies
subtitle: The newspaper method for merging a weak or queenless colony with a strong one.
excerpt: A weak colony going into winter rarely makes it through. One colony with 30,000 bees has a far better chance than two with 15,000 each. Combining before the frosts is a reliable way to save the bees from a colony you are not confident will survive on its own.
sourceNotes: National Bee Unit, Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), colony management in autumn. BBKA Technical Bulletin on uniting colonies. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive.

content.text: Combining two colonies is one of the most useful autumn tools a beekeeper has. A weak colony, a
, or one that has failed to build winter stores can be merged into a strong colony using the
method. The key principle is that the two colonies must be allowed to merge gradually, so they adopt a shared scent before meeting face to face.

You need one queen-right colony and one you want to absorb into it. The colony being absorbed may be queenless, or it may have a queen you are choosing to remove because she is failing. If the absorbed colony has a queen, remove and dispatch her first. You cannot combine two queen-right colonies; only one queen will survive, and the fight will be prolonged.

Move the weaker colony to sit next to the stronger one the day before you combine, if they are more than a metre apart. Bees find their way home to within a few metres of where the hive was. Moving a hive across the garden in one jump leaves many bees returning to the old spot.

Suit up and light the smoker. Remove the roof and crown board from the strong colony. Lay a single sheet of broadsheet newspaper over the top bars and prick several small holes through it with a hive tool; this hastens the chewing-through process without exposing the colonies to each other immediately.

Lift the entire brood box of the weaker colony (without its floor) and place it directly on top of the newspaper, on top of the strong colony's brood box. Replace the roof. The entrances to both boxes should face the same direction if possible; block the upper entrance with a small piece of foam or grass for the first day.

attrs.body: The bees from both colonies chew through the newspaper from both sides. By the time they meet in the middle, the colony scents have merged through the paper and flying has redistributed individual bees across both boxes. The merger is usually quiet. You will find shredded newspaper on the floor below the hive entrance.
attrs.title: What happens over the next 48 hours

content.text: After 3 to 4 days, open the hive and consolidate the two boxes into one. Move the best-covered frames of brood into the lower box and fill with the best honey stores. Any frames with poor brood pattern or mould can be set aside. The combined colony will sort itself out over the following fortnight; do a quick inspection at 10 days to confirm the queen is laying across the combined box.

entries.cue: Bees fighting at the entrance after combining
entries.fix: This is usually minor and short-lived. Add a light puff of smoke to the entrance. Ensure the entrance is small enough that fighting bees cannot form a large cluster outside it.
entries.cause: The newspaper was chewed through too quickly, or the holes were too large, meaning the bees met before scents had merged

entries.cue: Large clump of bees outside the combined hive after 24 hours
entries.fix: Move the hive slightly toward where the old position was. Open the entrance block you fitted on day one so they can get in. This usually resolves within a day as the bees recalibrate.
entries.cause: Bees from the absorbed colony are returning to their original position and clustering outside the hive entrance
